---
date: "2026-06-22"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
---

## A Text Number Is a Measurement, Not a Fact

**Learning objectives — by the end you can:**

- Treat any text-derived number as a noisy proxy
- Validate an instrument: gold set, held-out split
- Name the three ways AI fools you
- Disclose prompts, versions, and labels for reproducibility

::: notes
Welcome to Week Six, the mentor session — "Text as data, and AI without fooling yourself." This is part one of seven. For five weeks, data arrived already numeric — a return, a leverage ratio, a yield. This week the raw material changed under your feet: the data was prose. A ten-K running to fifty thousand words. A product description a firm wrote about itself. A risk-factor section a lawyer drafted to be read by no one. Today's whole discipline rests on one sentence, and in this opening part we earn it: a number you extracted from text is a measurement — a noisy proxy for something you cannot see directly.
:::

## The Raw Material Changed

- Five weeks: data arrived already numeric
- This week: data arrived as **prose**
- Prose must be *measured* into numbers first
- Ten-K filings, product descriptions, risk factors
- Then — and only then — it enters a regression

::: notes
Start with what shifted. For five weeks, when we said data, we meant numbers that showed up already numeric — a return, a leverage ratio, a yield. You read them straight off a database. This week, the data showed up as prose: a filing fifty thousand words long, a paragraph a firm wrote to describe its own products, a risk section drafted by a lawyer. Prose cannot enter a regression as text. It has to be measured into a number first — scored, classified, turned into similarity. And the instant you do that measuring, you have built an instrument, and every instrument has error. That is the hinge the whole session turns on.
:::

## The Seductive Illusion

- Point an instrument at a document, out comes a number
- Tone equals negative zero point zero four; label equals "negative"
- It *looks* like a fact — like the filing date
- It is not: it is an instrument's output, with error
- Better tools make the illusion **stronger**, not weaker

::: notes
Here is the trap, and it gets worse the better your tools get. You point an instrument — a dictionary, a similarity score, a large language model — at a document, and out comes a number. Tone equals negative zero point zero four. Similarity equals zero point three one. Label equals "negative." It looks like a fact about the document, the way the document's filing date is a fact. It is not. It is the output of an instrument, and like every instrument, it carries error. Recall the idea from Week One: when you write down "tone," you are not observing the filing's true sentiment. You are observing a noisy proxy for it — the true thing, plus a systematic bias, plus random noise. Re-run the model and the same filing might flip its label.
:::

## A Mismeasured Regressor

- True latent quantity + systematic bias + random noise
- The proxy enters a regression as a **mismeasured variable**
- Best case: coefficient attenuated toward zero, larger standard errors
- Worst case: error correlated with the model — biased, unsignable
- Right question: *how accurate, on my data, against truth?*

::: notes
Now connect it to what you already know. The instant that proxy enters a regression as a variable, it is a mismeasured variable — and you know what mismeasurement does. At best, classical measurement error attenuates your coefficient toward zero and inflates your standard errors. At worst, if the measurement error is correlated with something else in the model, it biases the estimate in a direction you cannot even sign. So the right question is never "is the language model accurate?" in the abstract. It is sharper than that: how accurate is this instrument, on my data, against truth — and is my downstream result robust to the error I know is there? Hold those two clauses. They are the spine of everything that follows.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
A text-derived number is a **measurement**, not a fact — a noisy proxy for a latent quantity, equal to truth plus systematic bias plus random noise. It enters a regression as a *mismeasured regressor*. The question is never "is it accurate?" but "how accurate, on my data, against truth — and does my result survive?" Next: the instruments, from transparent to opaque.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part one: a number you pull out of text is a measurement, not a fact. It equals the true latent quantity, plus a systematic bias, plus random noise — exactly the Week One picture, applied to a new sensor. Dropped into a regression, it is a mismeasured regressor: attenuated and noisy at best, biased and unsignable at worst. So stop asking whether the model is accurate in the abstract; ask how accurate it is, on your data, against truth, and whether your result survives the error you cannot remove. In part two, we line up the three instruments from this week and rank them from fully transparent to fully opaque.
:::
